summary: Four production patterns sit underneath every shipped agent: identity (who is asking), guardrails (what they can ask for), multi-tenancy hygiene (how their data stays separate), and tool publishing (how the agent finds the right capability). The default builder path runs without any of them by design – they're the seams you reach for once the prototype is real.

patterns.title: Identity
patterns.role: Who is the agent acting for, and on whose behalf
patterns.summary: AgentCore Identity gives the agent a workload identity ARN that distinguishes its own AWS calls from end-user calls. Pellier already routes anonymous shoppers through the namespace anon-{session_id} and signed-in shoppers through user-{cognito_sub}-session-{session_id} – Identity is the production-grade upgrade that lets the agent take an action on behalf of a specific customer without conflating the two.

namespacePattern.note: Same session id, different namespace – the switch is what AgentCore Memory keys on. Dashes (not colons) because AgentCore session IDs must match [a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9-_]*.

wiring.label: Cognito hosted UI
wiring.detail: Shopper signs in. Cognito redirects back with access_token + id_token in the URL hash.

wiring.label: Frontend stores token + session id
wiring.detail: AuthContext caches the access token in localStorage; chat service mints a stable session_id and persists it under pellier-session-id.

wiring.label: Outgoing request
wiring.detail: Every /api/agent/chat call sends Authorization: Bearer {access_token} plus the session id in the body. Subsequent turns echo the same id back.

wiring.label: Backend identity resolution
wiring.detail: AgentCoreIdentityService.get_verified_user_context() validates the JWT once per request via CognitoAuthService and resolves session_id from header → cookie → uuid4.

wiring.label: Namespace handed to AgentCore Memory
wiring.detail: build_namespace(user_id, session_id) returns user-{user_id}-session-{session_id} or anon-{session_id}. AgentCoreMemory.append_session_turn keys on the raw string – no merge on sign-in (Req 4.3.3).

patterns.codeSnippet:
# Workload identity for the agent itself
workload_identity_arn = settings.AGENTCORE_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_ARN

# OAuth2 credential provider for end-user calls
credentials = identity_client.get_token(
    workload_identity_arn=workload_identity_arn,
    user_token=cognito_id_token,
)

# Namespace switch — same session, different identity.
# Built once via AgentCoreIdentityService.build_namespace so the
# scheme lives in one place.
namespace = AgentCoreIdentityService.build_namespace(
    user_id=cognito_sub,  # None for anon shoppers
    session_id=session_id,
)
# → "user-{cognito_sub}-session-{session_id}" or "anon-{session_id}"
patterns.whatToReachFor: Identity becomes mandatory the first time you let the agent take an action that mutates a customer's data – orders, returns, profile edits. Until then, the namespace switch is the right boundary.

patterns.title: Guardrails
patterns.role: What the agent will and will not do, enforced at the edge
patterns.summary: Bedrock Guardrails layers content filters, denied-topic blocks, and PII redaction in front of every model call. Pellier's allow-list of categories and tags inside the StructuredExtractor is the same idea at the application layer – a whitelist that drops hallucinated values before they reach the SQL WHERE clause.
patterns.shippedNote: The Haiku-driven structured extractor whitelists categories and tags before they touch SQL – see services/structured_extract.py.

layers.name: Bedrock content filters
layers.what: Sexual / violence / hate / insults / misconduct / prompt injection – block or flag

layers.name: Denied topics
layers.what: Custom topic blocks (e.g. medical advice, legal advice)

layers.name: PII redaction
layers.what: Names, emails, phone numbers, addresses redacted before the response leaves the model

layers.name: Application allow-lists
layers.what: Categories and tags from Haiku are whitelisted against KNOWN_CATEGORIES / KNOWN_TAGS before they reach the SQL WHERE clause – hallucinated values are silently dropped

patterns.codeSnippet:
# Application-layer allow-list (already shipped)
cat_set = {c.lower(): c for c in KNOWN_CATEGORIES}
categories = [
    cat_set[c.lower()]
    for c in parsed.get("categories", []) or []
    if isinstance(c, str) and c.lower() in cat_set
]

# Bedrock Guardrail at the model boundary
response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId=settings.BEDROCK_HAIKU_MODEL,
    body=json.dumps(body),
    guardrailIdentifier="pellier-shopper-guardrail",
    guardrailVersion="1",
)

patterns.title: Multi-tenancy & STM hygiene
patterns.role: Keeping sessions, memories, and writes separate when traffic is real
patterns.summary: Every memory write, vector search, and tool call carries a namespace. STM is bounded (30-day expiry by design); long-term taste lives in Aurora pgvector profile embeddings keyed by user. Multi-tenancy hygiene is the operational discipline that keeps tenant A's data out of tenant B's recall – it's a property of the keys, not a separate system.
patterns.shippedNote: Namespaces flow through every memory call, agentcore_memory.append_session_turn, and pgvector profile embedding lookup.

concerns.answer: anon-{session_id} or user-{cognito_sub}-session-{session_id} – keyed at write time, scoped at read time.
30-day TTL on AgentCore Memory by design – the ledger is a recent record, not a permanent transcript.
Aurora pgvector profile embeddings, keyed by user id – not session id. Survives session expiry.
A vector search without a tenant filter returns rows from every tenant. The fix is in the WHERE clause, not the index – the index doesn't know about tenants.

patterns.codeSnippet:
# Session namespace at write time
ns = AgentCoreIdentityService.build_namespace(user_sub, session_id)
# → "user-{user_sub}-session-{session_id}" or "anon-{session_id}"
await memory.append_session_turn(
    namespace=ns,
    turn={"role": "user", "content": query},
)

# Tenant-safe vector search — predicate FIRST, then cosine
SELECT product_id, name,
       1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) AS similarity
FROM product_catalog
WHERE tenant_id = $2
  AND quantity > 0
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
LIMIT 5;

patterns.title: Tool publishing & discovery
patterns.role: How the agent finds the right capability when the catalog grows
patterns.summary: The default builder path uses in-process Strands tools – fast, type-safe, all in one Python process. At production scale, two surfaces help: AgentCore Gateway publishes tools over MCP so multiple agents share a single source of truth, and the Aurora pgvector tool registry lets a Strands agent semantically search tool descriptions when there are too many to fit in a system prompt.
patterns.shippedNote: Optional. The default Boutique path is in-process Strands tools – the orchestrator and tools live in one Python module.

surfaces.name: AgentCore Gateway (MCP)
surfaces.where: Configured via AGENTCORE_GATEWAY_URL – falls back to in-process when unset
surfaces.purpose: Lets multiple agents (re:Invent advanced track, ops tools, internal copilots) share one Lambda-backed tool surface. Each tool is published once, discovered over MCP, called by ARN.
surfaces.tradeoff: Adds a network hop and a deploy step. Worth it when more than one agent needs the same capability.

surfaces.name: Aurora pgvector tool registry
surfaces.purpose: Stores tool name + description + embedding. A Strands agent searches by semantic similarity to find the right tool when the catalog is too large for a static system prompt.
surfaces.tradeoff: Useful past ~20 tools. Below that, just put them in the prompt – the agent's tool-selection logic is the bottleneck, not retrieval.

patterns.codeSnippet:
# Optional Gateway path
if settings.AGENTCORE_GATEWAY_URL:
    agent = create_gateway_orchestrator()
else:
    agent = create_in_process_dispatcher()

# Aurora tool registry — semantic tool discovery
SELECT name, description,
       1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) AS similarity
FROM tools
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
LIMIT 5;
